content.full_text:
792149

County of San Diego

OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL

DONALD L. CLARK
County Counsel

JOSEPH KASE, JR.
Assistant County Counsel

355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
(714) 236-3651

FLOYD M. HARMON JR.
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL
DEPUTY

BETTY E. BOONE
LEONARDUDE
ANTHONY ALBERS
JOHN E. KING
B. KENT HARVEY
JOSEPH B. HUTCHINS
JOHN M. EVOY
WILLIAM HANSEN
WILLIAM H. VATES JR.
THOMAS J. CRAWFORD
ROBERT C. RICE
IVES A. HEBERT
GREGORY A. BARRETT

LEWIS F. ZOLLINGER
RICHARD J. BRADY
LEONARD DUCKWORTH
WILLIAM D. SMITH
JUDITH M. COPELAND
GEORGE H. JENSEN
ERNESTINE D. LITTLEJOHN
ROBERT C. LOWELL
PATRICIA L. STOLL
PHILLIP W. PRINCE
DIAZ OLSON
ARLENE PRATER

March 14, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

REQUEST FOR WRITTEN ADVICE
(GOV. CODE § 83114[b])

Honorable Commission:

This request for written advice is made pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b), which provides that
any person may request your Commission to provide
written advice with respect to his duties under the
Political Reform Act of 1974 (Section 81000 et seq.; all
references herein are to the Government Code unless
otherwise indicated). This request is made on behalf of
the Board of Supervisors of the County of San Diego, upon
whom said Act imposes the duty of reviewing Conflict of
Interest Codes of County agencies (§ 87303) and denying
approval of any such code if it fails to provide reasonable
assurance that all forseeable potential conflict of interest
situations will be disclosed or prevented (§ 87309[a]).

MATERIAL FACTS: Among the county agencies for which the
Board of Supervisors is the code reviewing
body are several Municipal Water Districts.
Several of these Districts retain private
attorneys as their regular counsel.

QUESTIONS PRESENTED:

1. Is such a privately-retained attorney required to
be included as a "designated employee" in the conflict of
interest codes of such Water Districts?

2. If such a person is to be included, is the
designation "consultant" or "staff" (employee/officer) more
appropriate?

Fair Political Practices
Commission                                    -2-                    March 14, 1978


3. What guidelines do you propose the code reviewing
body (and the agency) utilize in determining whether such
inclusion is required?

With regard to the "guidelines" we request, we are aware
of the following:

(a) Section 81100, prohibiting participation in
decision making where a "public official"
has a financial interest.

(b) Section 82048, defining "public official" to
include "consultants".

(c) Section 82019, defining "designated employee"
to include consultants.

(d) Section 18700(a)(2) of your Commission's
regulations, defining "consultant".

(e) Your Commission's Opinion No. 75-120
(August 3, 1976).

Unfortunately, the above are not sufficiently specific for
us to make the required determination in this particular case.

We are also aware of the existence of an advice letter
to Dr. Malcolm Richland, dated January 7, 1977. Although
we have not seen this letter, the Campaign Law Reporter
Reference Manual (Supplement No. 50) summarizes the conclusion
reached in said letter as follows:

"An agency legal counsel who for instance, is
available to provide legal services to the agency
purely on an ad-hoc basis, attends meetings only
upon specific request of the Board, is paid on an
hourly basis, and has no real or apparent authority
to act on behalf of the agency or bind it to a
specific course of action is not a consultant."

Would a change in any of these factors (e.g. regular attendance
of meetings or being paid an annual sum under a retainer
contract) change the result? Which factor(s) would you
consider most significant?

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,
DONALD L. CLARK, County Counsel

By [illegible]
WILLIAM W. TAYLOR, Deputy

WWT:an
cc: D. F. Shefte

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660                                322 5901                  322 6441                322 6444

April 10, 1978


William W. Taylor
Deputy County Counsel
San Diego County
355 County Administration Center
San Diego, California 92101

Dear Mr. Taylor:

     Thank you for your letter of March 14, 1978, requesting
advice with respect to whether attorneys for local agencies
should be included in the agency's conflict of interest code.
Initially, you should know that the Commission has included
attorneys as designated employees in virtually every code
which it has reviewed. Moreover, attorneys generally have
been included among the broadest disclosure categories for
the agency in question. This is because attorneys generally
have broad powers to affect decisions which could foreseeably
and materially affect their financial interests. These powers
include the authority to represent and bind the agency to a
course of action in court litigation, to counsel the agency
on litigation strategy and to advise the agency on the wisdom
and legal effects of its general policies.

     If you find that the attorneys in question possess such
powers, we recommend that they be designated employees in their
agency's conflict of interest codes. We think the focus is
properly placed on the attorney's decision-making authority
and not the fact of whether he is an employee as opposed to
a private practitioner who is retained by the agency. With
respect to whether the attorney should be designated as an
officer or a consultant, the Commission has accepted both
designations as appropriate. Therefore, you should make this
determination depending on the agency in question and the
attorney's role in that agency.

     With respect to the Richland letter, the Commission staff
found that the attorney in question was not a consultant be-
cause he could not bind the agency to a course of action and

William W. Taylor
April 16, 1978
Page Two


he was truly independent from supervision by the agency's
other employees. The factors cited in the Richland letter
were important to the staff as indicia of the fact that the
attorney in question was both independent and unable to make
or participate in the making of agency decisions. It is important to stress the fact that the situation described in
the Richland letter is unique and inapplicable to most agency
codes. Therefore, I would imagine that you will find the
functions performed by the attorneys retained by local agencies
in San Diego are sufficient to require that such persons be
designated in local codes.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

Sincerely,


Ted Prim
Chief, Conflicts of
Interest Division

sections.question:
include:
1. Is such a privately-retained attorney required to be included as a "designated employee" in the conflict of interest codes of such Water Districts?
2. If such a person is to be included, is the designation "consultant" or "staff" (employee/officer) more appropriate?

3. What guidelines do you propose the code reviewing body (and the agency) utilize in determining whether such inclusion is required?

With regard to the "guidelines" we request, we are aware of the following:

(a) Section 81100, prohibiting participation in decision making where a "public official" has a financial interest.

(b) Section 82048, defining "public official" to include "consultants".

(c) Section 82019, defining "designated employee" to include consultants.

(d) Section 18700(a)(2) of your Commission's regulations, defining "consultant".

(e) Your Commission's Opinion No. 75-120 (August 3, 1976).

Unfortunately, the above are not sufficiently specific for us to make the required determination in this particular case.

We are also aware of the existence of an advice letter to Dr. Malcolm Richland, dated January 7, 1977. Although we have not seen this letter, the Campaign Law Reporter Reference Manual (Supplement No. 50) summarizes the conclusion reached in said letter as follows:

"An agency legal counsel who for instance, is available to provide legal services to the agency purely on an ad-hoc basis, attends meetings only upon specific request of the Board, is paid on an hourly basis, and has no real or apparent authority to act on behalf of the agency or bind it to a specific course of action is not a consultant."

Would a change in any of these factors (e.g. regular attendance of meetings or being paid an annual sum under a retainer contract) change the result? Which factor(s) would you consider most significant?

Thank you for your attention to this matter.
sections.conclusion: If you find that the attorneys in question possess such powers, we recommend that they be designated employees in their agency's conflict of interest codes. We think the focus is properly placed on the attorney's decision-making authority and not the fact of whether he is an employee as opposed to a private practitioner who is retained by the agency. With respect to whether the attorney should be designated as an officer or a consultant, the Commission has accepted both designations as appropriate. Therefore, you should make this determination depending on the agency in question and the attorney's role in that agency.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether privately-retained attorneys for municipal water districts must be included as designated employees in conflict of interest codes, and if so, whether they should be designated as consultants or staff members, and what guidelines should apply to such determinations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC recommends including attorneys as designated employees if they possess broad decision-making authority to affect agency actions and decisions, focusing on their authority rather than employment status. Both officer and consultant designations are acceptable, depending on the specific attorney's role and the agency's circumstances.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Complete advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Document includes both the incoming request (dated March 14, 1978) and FPPC's response letter (dated April 10, 1978). Extraction confidence is high; minimal ambiguity in structure and content.

QUESTION: include:
1. Is such a privately-retained attorney required to be included as a "designated employee" in the conflict of interest codes of such Water Districts?
2. If such a person is to be included, is the designation "consultant" or "staff" (employee/officer) more appropriate?

3. What guidelines do you propose the code reviewing body (and the agency) utilize in determining whether such inclusion is required?

With regard to the "guidelines" we request, we are aware of the following:

(a) Section 81100, prohibiting participation in decision making where a "public official" has a financial interest.

(b) Section 82048, defining "public official" to include "consultants".

(c) Section 82019, defining "designated employee" to include consultants.

(d) Section 18700(a)(2) of your Commission's regulations, defining "consultant".

(e) Your Commission's Opinion No. 75-120 (August 3, 1976).

Unfortunately, the above are not sufficiently specific for us to make the required determination in this particular case.

We are also aware of the existence of an advice letter to Dr. Malcolm Richland, dated January 7, 1977. Although we have not seen this letter, the Campaign Law Reporter Reference Manual (Supplement No. 50) summarizes the conclusion reached in said letter as follows:

"An agency legal counsel who for instance, is available to provide legal services to the agency purely on an ad-hoc basis, attends meetings only upon specific request of the Board, is paid on an hourly basis, and has no real or apparent authority to act on behalf of the agency or bind it to a specific course of action is not a consultant."

Would a change in any of these factors (e.g. regular attendance of meetings or being paid an annual sum under a retainer contract) change the result? Which factor(s) would you consider most significant?

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

CONCLUSION: If you find that the attorneys in question possess such powers, we recommend that they be designated employees in their agency's conflict of interest codes. We think the focus is properly placed on the attorney's decision-making authority and not the fact of whether he is an employee as opposed to a private practitioner who is retained by the agency. With respect to whether the attorney should be designated as an officer or a consultant, the Commission has accepted both designations as appropriate. Therefore, you should make this determination depending on the agency in question and the attorney's role in that agency.
embedding.first_500_words: This is a letter from the Office of County Counsel for the County of San Diego, dated March 14, 1978. The letter is addressed to the Fair Political Practices Commission in Sacramento, California, and requests written advice regarding Government Code Section 83114(b). The request is made on behalf of the Board of Supervisors of the County of San Diego, who are responsible for reviewing conflict of interest codes of county agencies. The letter states that the Board of Supervisors is considering whether privately-retained attorneys for municipal water districts should be included as "designated employees" in the conflict of interest codes. It also asks whether the designation "consultant" or "staff" (employee/officer) would be more appropriate for such persons. The letter provides the following facts: - Among the county agencies for which the Board of Supervisors is the code reviewing body are several Municipal Water Districts. - Several of these Districts retain private attorneys as their regular counsel. Questions presented include: 1. Is such a privately-retained attorney required to be included as a "designated employee" in the conflict of interest codes of such Water Districts? 2. If such a person is to be included, is the designation "consultant" or "staff" (employee/officer) more appropriate? 3. What guidelines do you propose the code reviewing body (and the agency) utilize in determining whether such inclusion is required? With regard to the "guidelines" we request, we are aware of the following: (a) Section 81100, prohibiting participation in decision making where a "public official" has a financial interest. (b) Section 82048, defining "public official" to include "consultants". (c) Section 82019, defining "designated employee" to include consultants. (d) Section 18700(a)(2) of your Commission's regulations, defining "consultant". (e) Your Commission's Opinion No. 75-120 (August 3, 1976). Unfortunately, the above are not sufficiently specific for us to make the required determination in this particular case. We are also aware of the existence of an advice letter to Dr. Malcolm Richland, dated January 7, 1977. Although we have not seen this letter, the Campaign Law Reporter Reference Manual (Supplement No. 50) summarizes the conclusion reached in said letter as follows: "An agency legal counsel who for instance, is available to provide legal services to the agency purely on an ad-hoc basis, attends meetings only upon specific request of the Board, is paid on an hourly basis, and has no real or apparent authority to act on behalf of the agency or bind it to a specific course of action is not a consultant." Would a change in any of these factors (e.g. regular attendance of meetings or being paid an annual sum under a retainer contract) change the result? Which factor(s) would you consider most significant? Thank you for your attention to this matter. Sincerely, DONALD L. CLARK, County Counsel By WILLIAM W. TAYLOR, Deputy WWT:an cc: D. F. embedding.summary: The County Counsel of San Diego sought FPPC guidance on whether privately-retained attorneys for municipal water districts should be designated employees under conflict of interest codes. The FPPC responded that attorneys should be included as designated employees based on their decision-making authority to bind the agency and affect policy decisions, with the designation as officer or consultant depending on the attorney's specific role.
